title: HAICORE@KIT

verification.note: The legacy HAICORE pages and the HoreKa 2 migration page describe different access, architecture, and partition models. The facts below describe the legacy HAICORE endpoint and queues where the cited legacy pages are explicit. Before provisioning storage or submitting, confirm which system your account currently reaches and re-check the migration page, batch page, and support portal. Do not apply future HoreKa 2 or HAICORE 3 statements to the legacy service until the site marks them active for your account.

sources.title: Migration to HoreKa 2
sources.covers: transition timeline, architecture, access-model, and data-migration changes

sources.title: HAICORE interactive login
sources.covers: login endpoint, 2FA flow, allowed networks, and login-node boundary

sources.title: HAICORE batch system
sources.covers: legacy partitions, limits, Slurm fields, GRES names, and queue commands

sources.title: HAICORE hardware overview
sources.covers: legacy node topology, accelerators, local disks, and interconnect

sources.title: HAICORE file systems
sources.covers: HOME, workspaces, TMPDIR, BeeOND, lifetimes, snapshots, and backup

sources.title: HAICORE BeeOND
sources.covers: BEEOND constraint, job-local mount, capacity, and copy-out requirement

sources.title: HAICORE containers
sources.covers: Apptainer, Enroot, Pyxis, cache locations, and legacy mount examples

sources.title: HAICORE software modules
sources.covers: module discovery, loading, purging, and EasyBuild transition

requirements: Use the login username shown by FeLS, not an assumed institutional username.
The legacy service uses an OTP plus a dedicated service password.
Connect from an allowed network or through the VPN of your home institution.
Use the login node for file management, authoring, static planning, image preparation, and Slurm management. Run compute work only through a Slurm allocation.

accounts.name: assigned project/account
accounts.evidence: The batch page documents `--account=<group-name>` and says it may be needed when more than one group is assigned; it does not publish a universal account value.

partitions.name: normal
partitions.access: legacy default for users with access
partitions.limits: up to 1 node, 72 hours, and 4 full GPUs per job

partitions.name: advanced
partitions.limits: legacy page lists up to 10 nodes and 72 hours

qos.name: not confirmed
qos.hpc_compose: omit x-slurm.qos unless the site or your project gives a value
qos.evidence: No HAICORE-specific QOS name was published on the primary pages reviewed.

gres.name: full A100
gres.notes: The current legacy batch page explicitly requires the `full` type; for one GPU use `x-slurm.gres: gpu:full:1`.

gres.name: MIG 1g.5gb
gres.notes: Confirm the profile is still offered before submission.

gres.name: MIG 2g.10gb
gres.notes: Confirm the profile is still offered before submission.

gres.name: MIG 4g.20gb
gres.notes: Confirm the profile is still offered before submission.

runtimes.name: Pyxis / Enroot
runtimes.verification: `srun --help` must list `--container-image`; re-check the current site-required mounts.
runtimes.status: Documented on the legacy container page.

runtimes.name: Apptainer
runtimes.verification: Run `command -v apptainer` on the login node and verify it inside a small allocation.
runtimes.status: Documented as supported; Singularity was replaced by Apptainer in the legacy environment.

runtimes.name: Host modules
runtimes.verification: Use `module spider`, `module show`, and a finite allocation-backed smoke test.
runtimes.status: Available for software provided through modules; exact names are site state.

notes: Put required loads in `x-slurm.setup` so the batch environment is reproducible.
The HoreKa 2 transition page says the software environment moves to EasyBuild and module names differ; discover names on the actual target login node instead of copying legacy examples.
Do not leave a noisy `module avail` in production job scripts.

storage.name: $HOME
storage.hpc_compose_role: small configuration, source, and credentials under site policy
storage.avoid: large caches, datasets, or high-volume run output

storage.name: workspace
storage.lifetime: limited; legacy page says initially up to 60 days with at most 3 extensions
storage.hpc_compose_role: cache, recomputable data, run state, and checkpoint staging
storage.avoid: the only copy of durable results; workspace data is not backed up

storage.name: $TMPDIR
storage.hpc_compose_role: node-local temporary extraction or scratch inside a job

storage.name: BeeOND
storage.avoid: persistent cache, resume source, or data not copied out before job end

storage.name: LSDF
storage.visibility: login and compute nodes for users with access
storage.hpc_compose_role: durable or shared project data when your account is entitled
storage.avoid: assuming access without checking the site-specific environment and constraint

provisioning.title: Inspect or allocate the configured workspace through hpc-compose
notes: Configure the workspace name and site tools in `.hpc-compose/settings.toml` first.
`workspace release` is destructive and deliberately omitted from setup steps.

provisioning.title: Use the site tools directly when hpc-compose is not configured
notes: The legacy site page documents 60 days as the maximum initial lifetime; choose the shortest useful duration and confirm current limits.
Record the path returned by `ws_find`; do not guess it.

provisioning.title: Create the directories referenced by the spec
notes: A successful login-node write test is necessary but does not prove compute-node visibility; use the allocation-backed filesystem probe below before production.

cache_policy.cache: Put `x-slurm.cache_dir` on a globally visible workspace or other approved shared filesystem. Do not use `$TMPDIR`, `/tmp`, BeeOND, or another job-local path.
cache_policy.temporary: For Enroot extraction scratch, prefer a node-local path such as `/tmp/${USER}-hpc-compose-enroot` through `x-slurm.enroot_temp_dir`; keep the final image cache on shared storage.
cache_policy.durability: Treat workspace expiry as an operational deadline. Keep canonical checkpoints and exported results in an approved durable location, and rehearse copying them out before expiry or job teardown.
cache_policy.beeond: Request BeeOND with `x-slurm.constraint: BEEOND` only for job-local shared scratch and copy results to global storage before the allocation exits.

smoke_probes.name: Static plan
smoke_probes.expected: valid spec, understood lint findings, and the intended partition/account/GRES in the plan

smoke_probes.name: Login-node capability snapshot
smoke_probes.expected: the target partition is visible and the selected backend is discoverable

smoke_probes.name: Strict preflight
smoke_probes.expected: no blocking backend, mount, cache, or profile mismatch

smoke_probes.name: Shared-filesystem visibility
smoke_probes.expected: the compute-node probe can see and safely rename data on cache/runtime/resume/artifact paths

smoke_probes.name: Finite GPU smoke job
smoke_probes.expected: the requested device is visible and the finite assertion completes

smoke_probes.name: Distributed fabric smoke
smoke_probes.expected: requested MPI/NCCL probes complete on the actual runtime path

restrictions: Legacy `normal` is documented as single-node; do not submit a multi-node spec there. `advanced` needs explicit privilege.
The legacy page documents 5000 GPU-hours per user per year and at most 3 concurrently running jobs. These are quota facts, not reservations; re-check them during the migration.
A full GPU request is `gpu:full:N` on the current legacy batch page. Do not simplify it to `gpu:N` unless the target system's current page or `scontrol` output confirms that spelling.
No HAICORE-specific QOS name was confirmed. Omit `x-slurm.qos` rather than guessing.
The login pages and batch pages disagree on the number of login nodes behind the service. Use the documented endpoint and treat host count as an implementation detail.

The migration page describes x86 and ARM login/compute targets, new partitions, project-based HAICORE access, and data movement. Those future/transition facts are not silently substituted into this legacy guide.
Queue and idle-resource observations are advisory. They neither reserve resources nor guarantee that a job starts immediately.

common_failures.symptom: SSH never reaches the OTP prompt
common_failures.interpretation: wrong login username, disallowed network, or missing VPN
common_failures.checks: FeLS username, current network/VPN, then the support portal

common_failures.interpretation: legacy/new-system mismatch, missing advanced privilege, wrong account, or stale GPU spelling
common_failures.checks: migration page, `scontrol show partition`, rendered SBATCH directives, and support

common_failures.interpretation: Pyxis is unavailable on this target or the environment changed
common_failures.checks: `srun --help`, current container page, or switch only to a backend the site confirms

common_failures.symptom: Cache or bind path fails preflight
common_failures.interpretation: workspace expired, directory was never created, or the path is node-local

common_failures.symptom: Enroot import fails near squashfs creation with `Stale file handle`
common_failures.interpretation: extraction scratch may be on shared storage
common_failures.checks: move only `x-slurm.enroot_temp_dir` to node-local temporary storage; keep final cache shared

common_failures.symptom: Data disappears after the job or workspace expiry
common_failures.interpretation: BeeOND/TMPDIR was treated as durable or workspace copy-out was not completed
common_failures.checks: artifact export, canonical checkpoint path, workspace expiry, and durable destination

when: the legacy and migration pages do not identify which endpoint, partitions, account, or architecture applies to you
advanced access, account/project assignment, or a QOS value is required
documented runtime options or storage paths differ from live cluster behavior
a small, reproducible scheduler/backend/filesystem probe fails
include: verification date and source URLs consulted
target login endpoint and whether the system identifies as legacy or HoreKa 2
job id plus partition/account/GRES (never credentials or tokens)
the smallest redacted command and exact error
